structured_choices.data_source: Historical sensor data (temperature, vibration, pressure, etc.) collected at 1-minute intervals from production equipment over 36 months. Data sourced from the plant's SCADA system and maintenance information system (MIS). Data quality audit performed to identify gaps, sensor failures, and calibration drifts. Missing data patterns documented and addressed via forward-fill (max 4 hours) or equipment-removal for longer gaps. All sensor streams validated against maintenance logs for timestamp alignment.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary failure label defined as: equipment enters unplanned downtime state within 168 hours (7 days) of the observation window, as recorded in the MIS and cross-validated against production logs. Failures are identified by maintenance work orders flagged as 'unplanned' or 'emergency' with root cause codes indicating mechanical/electrical failure (not routine maintenance or operator error). A 7-day prediction horizon is chosen to align with maintenance crew scheduling constraints and stakeholder operational needs.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with equipment stratification. First 24 months (months 1–24) used for training. Months 25–30 used for validation. Months 31–36 held as test set. Within each split, data is stratified by equipment type (e.g., pump, compressor, motor) to ensure each class has representation across train/val/test. Temporal split prevents information leakage from future sensor readings into training; stratification ensures the model is not overfit to a single equipment cohort. Equipment IDs are not shuffled within splits to preserve temporal continuity of individual machines' degradation patterns.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (StandardScaler for sensor readings, SimpleImputer for missing values, categorical encoders for equipment type/location) are fit on training data only (months 1–24). These fitted transformers are then applied identically to validation (months 25–30) and test (months 31–36) without refitting. Outlier detection (IQR-based thresholding) is fit on training sensor distributions and applied to all splits. This prevents leakage of validation/test statistics into training.
structured_choices.features: Raw sensor features: rolling statistics (mean, std, max, min over 1-hour, 6-hour, and 24-hour windows) of temperature, vibration, and pressure. Derived features: rate-of-change of each sensor over 1-hour and 24-hour windows (to capture degradation trends). Equipment metadata: equipment type, age (days since commissioning), maintenance history count (number of prior failures in training window). External features: shift information (day/night), operator ID (as categorical). All rolling features computed per equipment instance to avoid cross-equipment contamination. No feature is computed using information from the 7-day future prediction window.
structured_choices.model: Gradient Boosting Regressor (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained to predict time-to-failure (TTF) in hours, where TTF ≤ 168 hours is later binarized as failure. The model outputs a continuous hazard score; this allows threshold optimization during validation. Hyperparameter tuning (learning rate, tree depth, regularization) performed on validation set using grid search over a predefined space. The regressor is chosen because it naturally handles non-linear relationships between sensors and failure, accommodates mixed feature types, and provides feature importance for interpretability.
structured_choices.baseline: Baseline 1 (Rule-based): Traditional maintenance schedule — predict failure if equipment age exceeds manufacturer-recommended maintenance interval (e.g., 2000 operating hours) or if prior maintenance was >X days ago. This is tuned by optimizing the interval threshold on validation data to maximize the primary metric (see below). Baseline 2 (Statistical): Logistic regression using the same feature set as the proposed model, with regularization tuned on validation data. Both baselines receive identical feature engineering, preprocessing, and validation effort as the proposed model. Baseline 1 establishes the operational status quo; Baseline 2 controls for whether gains are from the feature set vs. model complexity.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Downtime Cost Reduction: Defined as (Unplanned downtime hours prevented by model) × (Cost per downtime hour) − (False positive cost: unnecessary maintenance interventions). Operationally, this is proxied during evaluation as: (1 − FN_rate) × 100 − (FP_rate × maintenance_intervention_cost_ratio), where FN_rate = false negatives / all actual failures, FP_rate = false positives / all negatives. The threshold for binary classification is chosen to maximize this metric on the validation set. This metric directly reflects stakeholder objective: reduce unexpected production losses while avoiding excessive over-maintenance. Alternative parameterization: if cost data is unavailable, use F-beta score (β=2) to weight recall twice as heavily as precision, reflecting the higher cost of missed failures vs. unnecessary maintenance.
secondary_metrics: Recall (sensitivity) at predicted failure: What fraction of actual failures are flagged?
Precision: What fraction of flagged failures actually occur?
AUC-PR (area under precision-recall curve) — more informative than AUC-ROC under class imbalance
Mean time to detection (average hours before true failure that model generates alert)
False negative rate stratified by equipment type — ensures no single equipment class is neglected
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (months 25–30) is used exclusively for hyperparameter tuning and model selection. A grid search over hyperparameter space is performed; for each hyperparameter configuration, the model is trained on training data and evaluated on validation data using the primary metric. The configuration that maximizes the primary metric on validation data is selected. The test set (months 31–36) is held completely untouched during model selection and is evaluated exactly once, after the final model is locked.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is used for evaluation only, after all model selection and hyperparameter tuning are complete. A single final evaluation run is performed on months 31–36, with metrics reported on this held-out temporal window. No refitting, threshold reoptimization, or post-hoc adjustments are made after test set evaluation. Results reported include point estimates and 95% confidence intervals (computed via bootstrap or cross-validation on test data) for primary and secondary metrics.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Equipment utilization rate is captured as a feature (operating hours per day, derived from sensor activity logs) and included in the model to avoid conflation of high-failure rates with high-utilization environments. Operator ID is included as a categorical feature to account for skill-level effects; model importance scores are examined to check whether operator effects are material. Environmental conditions (ambient temperature, humidity if available) are collected and included. Equipment age is controlled via the 'days since commissioning' feature. A stratified analysis is performed: test-set performance is computed separately for each equipment type and high-vs.-low utilization cohorts. If significant performance degradation is observed in a subgroup (e.g., low-utilization equipment), this is flagged as a limitation. The experiment establishes whether sensor-based prediction outperforms schedule-based maintenance *on this facility's equipment mix*; generalization to different equipment types or deployment sites is not claimed without external validation.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Real-Time Sensor Prediction vs. Schedule-Based Maintenance

### 1. Data Collection and Quality
We would source 36 months of historical data from the plant's SCADA system and maintenance information system (MIS), comprising timestamped sensor readings (temperature, vibration, pressure) at 1-minute intervals and maintenance work orders. A data quality audit is performed upfront: we identify sensor gaps longer than 4 hours, calibration drifts, and cross-validate sensor timestamps against MIS records. Short gaps (<4 hours) are handled via forward-fill; equipment with longer outages during a month are excluded from that month's analysis. This ensures the training set reflects genuine operational data, not artifacts of system downtime.

### 2. Label Definition
Failure is defined operationally: equipment enters unplanned downtime (flagged in MIS as 'emergency' or 'unplanned maintenance') within 168 hours of an observation window. We use a 7-day horizon because this aligns with typical maintenance crew scheduling and gives stakeholders actionable lead time. Routine maintenance or operator-initiated shutdowns are excluded from the failure label to isolate true unexpected failures.

### 3. Train-Validation-Test Split
We employ a temporal split with equipment stratification. Months 1–24 form the training set, months 25–30 form the validation set, and months 31–36 are held as the final test set. Within each temporal window, data is stratified by equipment type (pump, compressor, motor, etc.) to ensure the model is exposed to failure patterns across all classes, preventing overfitting to a dominant equipment cohort. Temporal stratification prevents future information leakage and reflects the deployment scenario, where the model makes predictions on data chronologically forward of its training window.

### 4. Feature Engineering
We construct rolling statistics from raw sensors: mean, standard deviation, maximum, and minimum over 1-hour, 6-hour, and 24-hour windows. Rate-of-change features capture degradation trends (difference between consecutive 1-hour and 24-hour windows). Equipment metadata features include age (days since commissioning), prior failure count, equipment type, and maintenance history. External features include shift (day/night) and operator ID (to control for skill variation). All features are computed per equipment instance to avoid cross-equipment contamination. Importantly, no feature uses information from the future 7-day prediction window, eliminating lookahead bias.

### 5. Preprocessing and Fitting
All preprocessing transformers (StandardScaler, imputer, outlier detector) are fit exclusively on training data. These fitted transformers are applied without modification to validation and test sets. This prevents validation and test statistics from influencing training behavior.

### 6. Model and Baseline
We train a Gradient Boosting Regressor (XGBoost or LightGBM) to predict time-to-failure in hours; outputs are later binarized at a threshold optimized on validation data. The model handles mixed feature types and non-linear relationships naturally. Two baselines are provided:
- **Baseline 1 (Rule-based):** Traditional maintenance schedule—predict failure if equipment exceeds manufacturer-recommended maintenance interval or if prior maintenance exceeds X days. Threshold X is tuned on validation data.
- **Baseline 2 (Statistical):** Logistic regression with identical features and regularization tuned on validation data.

Both baselines receive equivalent engineering and tuning effort, ensuring fair comparison.

### 7. Validation and Model Selection
During model development, hyperparameters are tuned via grid search on the validation set (months 25–30). For each configuration, the model is trained on training data and evaluated on validation data. The configuration maximizing the primary metric is selected. The test set remains untouched.

### 8. Evaluation Metrics
The **primary metric** is Downtime Cost Reduction: (1 − FN_rate) × 100 − (FP_rate × intervention_cost_ratio), where FN_rate and FP_rate are measured on test data. This directly quantifies operational benefit: reduced unplanned downtime minus the cost of false-alarm interventions. If cost data is unavailable, we use F₂ score (β=2), weighting recall twice as heavily as precision to reflect the higher cost of missed failures.

**Secondary metrics** include recall, precision, AUC-PR, mean time to detection, and false negative rates stratified by equipment type. These provide diagnostic insight into which failure modes the model captures well and any blind spots by cohort.

### 9. Controls and Confounds
Equipment utilization rate (operating hours per day) is included as a feature to prevent conflation with failure rate. Operator ID and environmental conditions (where available) are also modeled. Post-hoc, we compute test-set metrics separately for each equipment type and utilization cohort to verify performance consistency. If significant degradation appears in a subgroup, this is flagged as a limitation. The experiment establishes superiority on this facility's equipment; generalization claims require external validation.

### 10. Test Set and Final Reporting
After model selection is complete, the test set (months 31–36) is evaluated exactly once. No retuning or post-hoc threshold adjustment occurs after this evaluation. Results are reported with point estimates and 95% confidence intervals for primary and secondary metrics.
